expectations.text: Session notes describe what happened in each of the 8 phases
expectations.evidence: session-notes.md contains explicit headings 'Phase 1 - Working Backwards', 'Phase 2 - Metrics Design', 'Phase 3 - Apply the Algorithm', 'Phase 4 - Probe for Gaps (sequential fallback)', 'Phase 5 - Verification Suite (TDD anchor)', 'Phase 6 - Draft', 'Phase 7 - Verify Adversarially (qa-agents simulated)', 'Phase 8 - Build Handoff and Final Assertions'. Each section has substantive content (gate outcomes, soft fails, findings).

findings.where: Phase 7 verification (Verification Record line 506) + A9
findings.what: qa-agents pass was simulated in-context by a single agent role-playing Finder/Auditor/Referee. The skill's Phase 7 is explicitly designed to use three independent agents to catch context-bleed and confirmation bias. Self-simulated adversarial review provides materially weaker assurance than independent agents, yet the spec was still promoted to status: verified on that basis.
findings.why_it_matters: A real Finder run with an independent context might have surfaced findings the simulated Finder rationalized away. The status: verified frontmatter signals 'this passed adversarial review' to downstream consumers (build agent, automation), but the assurance behind that label is weaker than the contract implies. A2 user trusting the verified label may underestimate risk.

findings.where: Phase 4 MECE fan-out (A8) + Procedure step coverage
findings.what: Phase 4's four MECE sub-agents ran sequentially in one context rather than as parallel independent agents. Sequential single-context probing is biased by earlier findings (anchoring) and cannot catch the same blind spots that four cold-start agents would. The spec acknowledges this in A8 but does not flag any specific finding category as therefore at risk.
findings.why_it_matters: Categories likely under-covered: 'Agent C - failure modes' typically benefits most from independent perspectives. The spec's failure-mode coverage may be biased toward failure modes Mike already mentioned (Greg ping-pong, figure drift) and miss orthogonal ones (e.g. agent prompt-injection from the milestones log, model-IP leakage in figure-provenance appendix).

findings.where: Decision Rules - 'Greg's response classification (greg_review)'
findings.what: Classification depends on natural-language parsing of Greg's reply ('looks good', 'approved', 'ship it'). No threshold is set for confidence, no human fallback is named for ambiguous replies that aren't 'approved with one tweak'. E.g. 'Mostly fine, but the runway figure feels off - can you double-check?' - is that a factual correction or a narrative edit? The spec's edge case 'corrections >0 vs edits >2' assumes the classifier reliably distinguishes the two.
findings.why_it_matters: If the classifier mislabels a hedged factual question as a narrative edit, the agent runs revise_narrative (no figure re-check), the figure stays stale, and the update ships wrong. This is precisely the bug class the spec exists to fix - figure-lock-before-draft solves the timing of figure pulls but does not solve classification fragility.

findings.where: Edge Cases - 'Financial model not closed for quarter' AND Preconditions
findings.what: Preconditions states 'The financial model has been closed for the quarter (book-close complete)'. Edge Cases handles 'not closed' via abort_book_not_closed. But there is no detection mechanism for a partially-closed model (some cells closed, others not). The kickoff Decision Rule looks for a 'BOOK_CLOSED_<quarter>' cell or Mike's word, which is binary - it doesn't detect the realistic case where book-close is in progress and some KPI cells are stable while others aren't.
findings.why_it_matters: Mike says 'yes book is closed' on Monday because revenue is closed; cash isn't reconciled until Wednesday. lock_figures runs Monday, snapshots cash from a not-yet-closed reconciliation, lock_figures stability check passes (cell hasn't been edited in 48h because nobody's touching it), figures ship wrong. The 48h-since-last-edit heuristic is a stability proxy, not a closure proxy.

findings.where: Output - figure-provenance appendix + Build Notes Out-of-scope
findings.what: Output spec says appendix contains 'cell references back to the financial model' delivered as an attachment. No redaction or scoping rule is named for the appendix. The financial model likely contains figures, formulas, and structure that GIX investors should NOT see (e.g. forward forecasts, uncited operating costs, employee comp, other LP details). 'Cell URL + value + as-of date' could leak sheet structure or per-cell formulas if the appendix format isn't tightly constrained.
findings.why_it_matters: An investor clicks the Drive cell-URL link in the appendix, lands in a sheet they're not authorized to see (Drive ACL works in their favor, but sheet layout is exposed in screenshots). Or the appendix template inadvertently includes a formula text. Information disclosure to LPs of competitively sensitive data is a real failure mode for an investor-update process.

findings.where: A10 (Open question) - securities-law / forward-looking statements review
findings.what: Open Question A10 acknowledges there's no compliance/legal review gate for forward-looking statements, performance claims, or securities-law language. This is flagged as 'Decision needed before first production run' - meaning the spec was promoted to status: verified with a known compliance gap that could materially affect production use.
findings.why_it_matters: An agent-generated forward-look section could include statements that, in a private placement or LP context, trigger Reg D safe-harbor issues or implied performance guarantees. Mike sends, content is bad, GIX or counsel surfaces it months later. A spec with status: verified that flags this as 'TBD before production' is contradictory - either the compliance gate is in scope (then add it) or the spec is verified-for-design-only (then status should reflect that).

findings.where: Procedure step 11 greg_review SLA timer + Build Notes
findings.what: SLA is '3 business days', Build Notes specifies 'wall-clock business-day timer (skip weekends and US federal holidays)'. No definition of which holiday calendar (US federal vs. NY exchange vs. company-specific). Greg may live in a different timezone or country - 'business day' isn't defined relative to whose calendar.
findings.why_it_matters: Edge case where Greg is traveling and a US-only holiday extends an effective SLA, or Greg's local Friday holiday is a US business day and the timer expires unfairly. Low impact because Mike-override is named, but worth pinning.

findings.where: Verification Suite - 'Highlight selection is correctly attributed to Mike'
findings.what: This semantic check is classified as agent-verified by inspection. The check name is concrete now (post-fix) but the verification method - 'agent reads spec and confirms' - is exactly the kind of self-judging verification that the qa-agents pattern exists to harden. There's no external script or test that fails if a future spec edit silently moves highlight-selection ownership to the agent.
findings.why_it_matters: Spec rot: a future maintainer 'optimizes' select_highlights to be agent-decided to save a Mike interruption, the semantic check still passes on agent self-inspection, the highlight-Mike-judgment intent is lost. Low because Mike will likely notice the first run, but a structural assertion (e.g. 'Requirement Owners table for select_highlights row contains the literal string Mike') would be stronger.

findings.where: Parallelization section + Phase 5 diagram justification
findings.what: Parallelization section says gather_milestones, load_prior_update, apply_prior_feedback can run in parallel after lock_figures. The Mermaid diagram and Procedure successors render them as a strict sequential chain S4 -> S5 -> S6. The diagram doesn't reflect the stated parallelism. Build agent reads the diagram first, implements sequential, parallelization opportunity is lost.
findings.why_it_matters: Minor cycle-time optimization missed; spec is internally inconsistent (diagram and Parallelization section disagree). Build Notes does say 'build agent decides' but the diagram is more directly authoritative for most implementers.

findings.where: Verification Record line 506
findings.what: Verification Record claims 'Auditor disprovals: 3 (F1, F3, F6)' and 'Referee net: 3 confirmed real findings (F2 metric gap, F4 drafting, F5 edge-case gap)'. The arithmetic in session-notes adds up, but the Referee's role per qa-agents is to break Finder/Auditor disagreements - if Auditor disproved F1, F3, F6 cleanly, did Finder accept the disprovals or did Referee adjudicate? The record reads as Auditor's word being final, which conflates Auditor and Referee in the simulation.
findings.why_it_matters: Audit-trail clarity. If a future reviewer wants to second-guess whether F3 (time_to_investors_days threshold) was really disproved, the record doesn't show whether Finder agreed with the disproof or Referee was needed. Low impact, but a real qa-agents three-agent run would have an explicit Finder-rebuts-Auditor exchange or an Auditor-uncontested note.

No 'use judgment' language inside Decision Rules. (Note: Build Notes does say 'Use judgment on...' for implementation choices, but that's outside the Decision Rules section.)

expectations.text: Session notes describe each of the 8 phases
expectations.evidence: session-notes.md headings: 'Phase 1 - Working Backwards', 'Phase 2 - Metrics Design', 'Phase 3 - Apply the Algorithm', 'Phase 4 - Probe for Gaps', 'Phase 5 - Verification Suite', 'Phase 6 - Draft + Gate 6', 'Phase 7 - Adversarial QA', 'Phase 8 - Build Handoff'. All 8 phases described.

findings.where: Inputs section, calendar input (lines 62-66) + Session notes Phase 6 fail #2 (lines 56-57)
findings.what: The author admits in session-notes that calendar was reordered to the end of the Inputs list specifically to dodge a regex bug in verify_spec.py's controllable-input check. The spec inserts a parenthetical at line 62 explaining the reordering as a structural property of the spec itself ('Listed last because it is the only non-controllable input - the script's controllable-input check is lazy-matched'). This pollutes the canonical artifact with grader-implementation detail to satisfy the structural grader; the spec is now coupled to the verifier's bug rather than to the process. A reader has no reason to know or care about the ordering rationale.
findings.why_it_matters: Specs should describe the process; verifier-conformance commentary is meta-noise that misleads downstream readers (and the build agent) about which constraints are real. If verify_spec.py is fixed, this comment becomes a stale lie.

findings.where: Verification Record (lines 391-395) and session-notes Phase 7 (lines 61-89)
findings.what: Both QA Agents 'runs' were simulated inline by the same agent that drafted the spec ('Task tool unreachable -> followed SKILL.md scoring rubric directly'). The author's own skill-improvement note #3 (session-notes line 146) acknowledges this collapses the adversarial-isolation premise: 'the same agent that drafted F1-F12 also disproved them'. Yet the spec still claims status: verified on the strength of these self-graded runs. The Verification Record reads as if independent adversarial review occurred when it did not.
findings.why_it_matters: Adversarial review only works when the auditor has no investment in the artifact's correctness. A self-simulated qa-agents pass is at best a self-edit checklist; treating it as Verification Record evidence inflates the spec's apparent rigor and could let real defects through. The status: verified label rests partly on this fictive validation.

findings.where: Decision Rule DR1 (line 228)
findings.what: DR1's threshold is 'fuzzy match with similarity >=0.85 under the cosine-similarity-on-OpenAI-text-embedding-3-small-embeddings convention (the build agent may swap the embedding model but must record the choice in the spec's Build Notes and re-tune the threshold).' Allowing the build agent to swap the embedding model AND re-tune the threshold means DR1 effectively reduces to 'pick a model and a threshold' - the canonical 0.85 number is decorative. Two builds of this spec would produce non-comparable commitments_carried_forward_pct values, defeating the DMAIC measurement premise.
findings.why_it_matters: This violates the assertion 'Decision rules resolve on input alone' in spirit even if it passes in letter. Build-time variability in core thresholds means the metric isn't actually deterministic across runs/builds, and trend analysis in the Metrics Review Plan becomes unreliable.

findings.where: Edge Cases table, 'TODO secretly rewritten' row (line 261)
findings.what: Handling specifies a per-TODO history JSONL keyed by 'stable source-id (Asana task GID, vault file:line, etc.)'. But 'vault file:line' is NOT a stable identifier - any insertion above the TODO shifts every subsequent line number. The supposed stable key is the failure mode it's meant to detect (rewritten/moved TODOs).
findings.why_it_matters: The history-diff mechanism that backs identify.stuck for vault TODOs will silently misclassify any TODO that moved in its file as 'new', losing the entire purpose of the stuck-but-rewritten detection. This is a real bug seeded directly into the build handoff.

findings.where: Mermaid diagram, Gate2 retry handling (lines 298-301)
findings.what: Gate2 has edges: 'fail and retries < 1' -> S6 (re-draft), 'fail and retries == 1' -> S7, 'pass' -> S7. The retry counter increment is not shown in the diagram - there is no edge that increments 'retries'. The diagram implies the counter springs into existence between traversals. The Gates table (line 210) says enforcement is 'by an integer counter in the orchestrator state, not by convention' - but the diagram doesn't model that state transition.
findings.why_it_matters: Diagram-spec mismatch. A build agent reading the diagram alone can't know how to implement the retry budget; reading the table contradicts the diagram. The 'no unbounded loops' verifier check passed only because the diagram structurally has finite outgoing edges, not because the retry semantics are correctly modeled.

findings.where: DR2 + Inputs (calendar Required: no, line 64)
findings.what: DR2 defines drift-thread proportion using tracked_hours = calendar event durations + (completed_items count * 1-hour proxy when calendar degraded). When calendar IS available, completed_items contribute 0 hours unless they appear on the calendar. So a deep-work week with no meetings would show 0 tracked_hours total -> 0/0 division -> drift detection breaks. The degraded fallback only kicks in when calendar is fully unavailable, not when calendar is available-but-empty.
findings.why_it_matters: A common real-world scenario (focused solo week, light meeting load) breaks the drift detector silently. There's no edge case for 'calendar available but sparse'.

findings.where: Output Metrics table (line 87) and Process Health (line 122)
findings.what: review_cycle_time_min is listed as an Output Metric ('end of ratify.and.write') AND End-to-end cycle time is listed as a Process Health metric ('Wallclock from session start to file written. Target <=45 min, hard cap 75 min.'). These are the same metric in two categories. The Metrics Map should not double-count.
findings.why_it_matters: Minor taxonomy issue but suggests the Metrics Map categories aren't cleanly separated, which weakens the DMAIC analysis.

findings.where: Edge Cases, 'Mike walks away mid-ask.subjective' (line 267)
findings.what: Auto-fill triggers at 20 min wallclock with no input, but ask.subjective is the step that emits subjective_state for probe.drift's clause (iii). Auto-filling with '(skipped - Mike unavailable)' and specificity_score=0 means probe.drift can't fire its third trigger condition - degraded mode is implicit but not flagged in the artifact. The 20-min number is also unjustified vs. the 30-45 min total budget - a 20-min subjective wait alone consumes nearly half the target cycle time before timing out.
findings.why_it_matters: Threshold seems arbitrary and interacts poorly with the cycle-time budget. Also, auto-filling silently weakens drift detection without explicit downstream flagging.

findings.where: Assumptions and Open Questions A8 (line 387)
findings.what: A8 says Phase 4 sub-agents weren't reachable so probes ran sequentially. This is process-design SKILL execution metadata, not a property of the weekly-review process being designed. It should be in session-notes (where it also appears), not in the spec itself. Conflates skill-trace with deliverable.
findings.why_it_matters: Pollutes the canonical spec with operational detail about how it was authored. Future readers building from this spec don't need to know the meta-process trace.

findings.where: Verification Suite (line 330)
findings.what: Last row says 'Output metric commitments_carried_forward_pct is computable from inputs alone | structural | script (process-specific extension to verify_spec.py: ...)'. But this extension does not exist - verify_spec.py is the shared script and the spec promises a feature that hasn't been built. The check is effectively unverified.
findings.why_it_matters: Lists a verification check that won't actually run. Either build the extension or downgrade to 'agent semantic check'.

expectations.text: Cadence is named (Friday or weekly) somewhere in the spec
expectations.evidence: Line 4: '**Cadence:** Weekly, Friday 3:00–3:45 PM (45-minute hard stop)' — both 'Weekly' and 'Friday' explicitly named

expectations.text: Output is a written review artifact (markdown / document), not a verb
expectations.evidence: Section F line 91 specifies saving as 'weekly-review-YYYY-MM-DD.md' in the vault; the template file is structured as a markdown artifact; §10 says 'Each weekly review is a note in the vault'

expectations.text: Inputs include both the week's completed activity AND prior-week commitments
expectations.evidence: §3 Inputs table includes both completed activity (Calendar last 7 days, Closed PRs/commits, Asana tasks closed this week, vault deltas) AND prior-week commitments ('The 3 commitments you made *to yourself* last Friday' and 'Last week's review file')

expectations.text: Process Health or Output metrics include at least one drift-detection signal (e.g. % commitments completed, focus-area continuity score, week-over-week velocity)
expectations.evidence: Metric 1 'Top-3 Hit Rate' = (Done + 0.5 × Partial) / 3 is exactly a % commitments completed signal; Metric 2 stuck-item age tracks portfolio drift; Metric 3 hygiene score is a process-drift signal

expectations.text: Mermaid block has at least 5 step nodes plus at least one GATE decision node
expectations.evidence: No Mermaid diagram present anywhere in weekly-review-process.md or template — baseline used markdown headings/tables instead of a flowchart

expectations.text: Edge Cases include 'commitment slipped' or equivalent recurring failure mode
expectations.evidence: §7 'Anti-patterns' covers recurring failure modes including 'Aspirational top-3' (commitments slip because they were aspirational) and 'Stuck-item amnesia' (slipping items disappear); §6 trigger 'Top-3 hit rate red 2 weeks in a row' is the explicit commitment-slipped recurring-failure handler. Section A also explicitly scores commitments as Done/Partial/Not done/Abandoned.

expectations.text: Build handoff file exists and references the chosen target with appropriate capture mechanisms
expectations.evidence: No build-handoff file produced. session-notes.md is a design-rationale doc, not a build handoff; §8 references a future weekly-review-prep.sh but doesn't constitute a handoff file

expectations.text: Decision rules resolve on input alone (no 'use judgment' or vague criteria)
expectations.evidence: Metric thresholds are concrete numbers (≥0.66 green, 0.34–0.65 yellow, ≤0.33 red; P50 ≤21 days; hygiene ≥3 green); §6 trigger conditions are all input-resolvable ('red 2 weeks in a row', 'same item red 3 weeks running', 'all three green for 8 consecutive weeks'); 'Done means a customer/teammate/system other than you experienced the change' is concrete. Hygiene rubric is binary +1 checks. No 'use judgment' language.

expectations.text: Session notes describe each of the 8 phases
expectations.evidence: session-notes.md describes the baseline's own design approach and decisions but does not describe 8 phases of the process-design plugin's workflow — baseline never ran an 8-phase workflow

expectations.text: Spec file written to the expected output path
expectations.evidence: File exists at outputs/drive-manager-rule-update.process-spec.md

expectations.text: verify_spec.py on the spec exits 0
expectations.evidence: Ran verify_spec.py without --final: 32 passed, 0 failed; exit code 0

expectations.text: verify_spec.py --final on the spec exits 0
expectations.evidence: Ran verify_spec.py --final: 40 passed, 0 failed; exit code 0

expectations.text: Frontmatter has status: verified
expectations.evidence: Line 7: 'status: verified'

expectations.text: Frontmatter has build_target: python-script
expectations.evidence: Line 8: 'build_target: python-script'

expectations.text: Trigger condition for the feedback loop is explicit (e.g. user moves file post-routing, or user flags mis-route)
expectations.evidence: Inputs section defines mis_route_signal from two explicit sources: '(a) FS-watcher that observed a file move from a drive-manager-placed location within STALE_THRESHOLD_HOURS (default 168h) of placement, or (b) explicit drive-manager:flag-mis-route <path> invocation'

expectations.text: Output describes an updated routing rules artifact (file, table, or PR), not a re-routed file
expectations.evidence: Output anchor: 'An updated ~/.claude/plugins/drive-manager/routing-rules.yaml file with one new (or modified) rule' plus a 'diff artifact' for human review — the artifact is the rules file, not a re-routed file

expectations.text: Procedure has at least one decision point that distinguishes 'flaky route, no rule change needed' from 'systemic miss, rule should change'
expectations.evidence: Decision Rules section: 'Is the proposed rule a duplicate, conflict, or unique?' — duplicate path leads to log_learning_event_only (no rule write); unique/conflict leads to regression_replay then write_proposal_diff. Also 'User intentionally moved file (re-organizing, NOT correcting a route)' edge case distinguishes flaky/incidental signals from real mis-routes via FAST_CORRECTION_HOURS window. dedupe_against_rules step explicitly classifies as duplicate vs. conflict vs. unique

expectations.text: Mermaid block has at least 5 step nodes plus at least one GATE decision node
expectations.evidence: Mermaid has 15+ step nodes (S1–S15) plus 2 GATE decision nodes (G1: Gate-SignalValid, G2: Gate-HumanApproval), plus Start/terminal end nodes. Far exceeds the threshold.

expectations.text: Edge Cases include conflicting signals (two corrections disagree) or equivalent
expectations.evidence: Edge Cases table includes 'Two agents reach this design from different mis-routes simultaneously' and dedupe_against_rules handles 'conflict' (same match.pattern + different destination). Also 'User moves file twice (A→B→C)' handles successive disagreeing moves via T_settle defer.

expectations.text: Build handoff names Python-specific capture mechanisms (decorators, context managers, structured logging)
expectations.evidence: build-handoff.md line 11: 'Use decorators, context managers, structured logging libraries (e.g., structlog) for telemetry.' Spec Build Notes line 499 also names: 'Python decorators or context managers wrapping each step function; structured logging via structlog or stdlib logging with a JSONL formatter.'

expectations.text: Decision rules are testable on input alone
expectations.evidence: All five decision rules have concrete criteria evaluable from inputs: signal staleness uses observed_at - placed_at >= 168; original log existence uses file_hash or file_path+timestamp; dedupe uses pattern+destination+content_predicate equality; regression uses pass_count/count >= 0.98; human approval uses filesystem move into approved/ folder. No subjective evaluation required.

expectations.text: Session notes describe each of the 8 phases
expectations.evidence: session-notes.md has explicit 'Phase 1 — Working Backwards' through 'Phase 8 — Build Handoff' headers, each with summary content (Gate pass/fail, soft fails count, key outputs). All 8 phases are described.

findings.where: Procedure step 12 apply_rule_on_approve + diagram edge S12 -->|hash race or lock timeout| S15
findings.what: Hash-race detection is supposed to retry from regression_replay (per Edge Cases table: 'Routing-rules.yaml modified mid-run ... Abort the apply; re-run from regression_replay against the new rule set; if still passes, retry apply'), but the procedure successors and the mermaid diagram both route hash-race and lock-timeout straight to abort_invalid. The two specifications contradict each other.
findings.why_it_matters: Implementer following the procedure/diagram will give up on transient YAML conflicts when a re-replay-then-retry was the spec's intent. Either the edge case is wrong or the procedure is — the build agent has no canonical tiebreaker.

findings.where: Decision Rule 'Did regression_replay pass?' vs Output Metrics row 'replay_correctness_rate'
findings.what: regression_replay's pass criterion requires 'regression_replay_pass_count / regression_replay_count >= 0.98 AND target file routes to corrected_destination'. But the success criterion in the Output anchor sets two thresholds: replay_correctness_rate ≥ 95% on target file and regression_rate ≥ 98% on history. The 95% target threshold is meaningless for a single-file binary metric (it's either 0 or 1) — the spec mixes per-event binary scoring with a population threshold.
findings.why_it_matters: Implementer cannot tell whether to compute replay_correctness_rate as a per-event 0/1 (as Metrics Map says) or as a population rate (as the success criterion's 95% implies). This will cause divergence between the metric written to learning-events.jsonl and the metric used to judge process health.

findings.where: Inputs / mis_route_signal validation
findings.what: The signal validation requires both destinations resolve under specific drive roots, listing only Google Drive, Linglepedia, and iCloud. But the existing drive-manager skill (per its own SKILL.md) supports both Google Drives (plural — multiple accounts per the Google MCP two-account split memory) and iCloud Drive. Hardcoding paths via the wildcard 'GoogleDrive-*' is fine, but Linglepedia is just one folder under iCloud — listing it separately as a 'drive root' is a category error and will cause valid corrected_destinations under non-Linglepedia iCloud paths to look like they are. Actually the iCloud path is listed too, so this is OK — but the Linglepedia path is technically inside iCloud and the order matters for resolution.
findings.why_it_matters: Edge case is minor but shows the spec's drive-root taxonomy hasn't been verified against the actual drive-manager plugin's known roots — this is exactly the A1 assumption flagged for build-time verification, but the validation logic is being committed to procedurally.

findings.where: Edge Cases — 'User intentionally moved file (re-organizing, NOT correcting a route)'
findings.what: The decision criterion uses FAST_CORRECTION_HOURS=6 to distinguish corrections from re-organization, but this contradicts the main signal validation which uses STALE_THRESHOLD_HOURS=168 (1 week). Signals between 6h and 168h fall into a gray zone: stale_threshold says 'fresh enough to be a correction' but the edge-case rule says 'too late to be auto-trusted, route to log_learning_event with outcome=signal_low_confidence'. The procedure has no step that actually applies this 6h gate — it lives only in prose in the Edge Cases table.
findings.why_it_matters: A whole class of signals (6h–168h via fs_watcher) silently bypasses the proposal pipeline with no step in the procedure or successor edge in the diagram showing the divergence. Implementer either misses the gate or hand-codes it in violation of the canonical procedure.

findings.where: Procedure step 5 load_rules — input list
findings.what: load_rules now lists process_inbox_log as an input (per the Pass 2 fix routing the effectiveness metric there) but does not declare a fallback successor for the case where process_inbox_log is missing or corrupted. extract_signals declares 'if file missing: → abort_invalid' but load_rules' equivalent path (log missing or unparseable) only handles 'YAML parse error'.
findings.why_it_matters: Minor — degraded mode for missing log is documented in the input validation, but the Procedure successor list doesn't reflect it.

findings.where: Verification Suite — semantic checks routed to 'agent'
findings.what: Three semantic checks ('Decision rules resolve on input alone', 'Output is concrete', 'Spec matches design conversation intent') are listed as agent-verifiable but no agent invocation is referenced and no record of pass/fail appears in the Verification Record. The Verification Record only logs qa-agents passes, not these specific semantic checks.
findings.why_it_matters: The spec promises an agent verifies these but provides no audit trail. Reader cannot tell if they were actually run.

findings.where: Decision Rule 'Did the human approve?' / Gate-HumanApproval polling
findings.what: Gate-HumanApproval polls 'proposals/approved/ and proposals/rejected/' every loop iteration with REVIEW_TIMEOUT_HOURS=168. There's no specification of poll frequency, no spec for what happens if the script exits before approval (does a daemon resume polling?), and no behavior for the 168h-but-script-was-killed-after-1h scenario. The spec implicitly assumes a long-running process but the build target is 'python-script' (singular run).
findings.why_it_matters: Build target / runtime model mismatch. A python-script that polls for 168 hours either leaks a process or requires a separate daemon/scheduler the spec doesn't mention. The implementer must invent a runtime contract.

findings.where: Output anchor — 'rule_acceptance_rate ≥ 95%' implication
findings.what: Output success criterion says replay_correctness_rate ≥ 95% (binary per-event metric), but the Metrics Review Plan expects 'rule_acceptance_rate: expect 70±15% steady-state'. The two are different metrics but the framing is inconsistent — the success criterion's 95% bar will never trip because it's a per-event binary; the operational metric of interest is rule_acceptance_rate which is set at 70%.
findings.why_it_matters: Mostly a clarity/drafting issue but compounds with finding #2.

findings.where: Build Notes — 'Stdlib + PyYAML only — no LLM dependency in v1'
findings.what: Constraint says no LLM, but propose_rule's heuristic ('match (filename_glob and/or pattern derived from filename, weighted toward generic over literal), content_predicate (a keyword set drawn from extracted signals)') is non-trivial and the spec gives no algorithmic detail. 'Weighted toward generic' is hand-wavy.
findings.why_it_matters: Implementer will either invent a weighting scheme that may not match Mike's expectations, or kick this back as ambiguous. The Pass 2 finding noted heuristic underspecification but routed it to drafting; arguably the propose_rule algorithm warrants its own decision rule.

findings.where: Procedure / dedupe_against_rules dedupe + content_predicate logic
findings.what: Decision Rules say `duplicate` requires same pattern + same destination + same content_predicate. But dedupe_against_rules step description says 'scan for any rule whose match.pattern exactly equals proposed_rule's, regardless of destination. If equal pattern + same destination: skip ... If equal pattern + different destination: emit conflict'. The step omits content_predicate from the comparison; the decision rule includes it. This is a real spec/procedure mismatch.
findings.why_it_matters: Dedup classification will produce different results depending on which rule the implementer follows. Two rules with same pattern+destination but different content_predicates are 'duplicate' per step but 'unique' per decision rule (or vice versa).

expectations.text: Spec file written to the expected output path
expectations.evidence: SPEC.md exists at outputs/SPEC.md with full meta-process content (170 lines).

expectations.evidence: Ran verify_spec.py against outputs/SPEC.md — exit code 1. FAIL: 'YAML frontmatter parses — missing or malformed YAML frontmatter'. Baseline produced no YAML frontmatter at all.

expectations.evidence: Ran verify_spec.py --final — exit code 1, same frontmatter-missing failure. Final-mode requires status: verified, which is also absent.

expectations.text: Frontmatter has status: verified
expectations.evidence: No YAML frontmatter block exists in SPEC.md. The string 'status: promoted' appears in line 109 inside a JSON example, but there is no frontmatter status field.

expectations.text: Frontmatter has build_target: python-script
expectations.evidence: No YAML frontmatter exists. The spec does describe a Python script as the build target in §5 narratively, but the structural frontmatter field is absent.

expectations.text: Trigger condition for the feedback loop is explicit (e.g. user moves file post-routing, or user flags mis-route)
expectations.evidence: §3.1 DETECT explicitly lists two trigger paths: (1) live signal — fswatch detects file moved away from skill's chosen path via route-decision.jsonl audit log diff, and (2) manual signal — user runs `python feedback_loop.py log-correction <file>`. Both are concrete and explicit.

expectations.text: Output describes an updated routing rules artifact (file, table, or PR), not a re-routed file
expectations.evidence: §3.4 PROMOTE is explicitly about editing rules.yaml with a provenance block, single semantic operations, and a git commit / timestamped backup. The output of the loop is a rule edit (with example YAML rule shown lines 97-107), not a re-routed file. §8 reinforces: 'It only edits rules; the skill still does the routing.'

expectations.text: Procedure has at least one decision point that distinguishes 'flaky route, no rule change needed' from 'systemic miss, rule should change'
expectations.evidence: §3.3 TRIAGE has a 4-bucket classifier (A. New rule, B. Strengthen existing, C. Disambiguation needed, D. One-off / noise) with bucket D explicitly defined as 'genuinely unusual — wedding photo dropped in by mistake, etc. Not a pattern. Mark rejected. Do not touch rules.' This is the exact flaky-vs-systemic distinction. The recurrence-check heuristic (≥2 in 90 days) reinforces it.

expectations.text: Mermaid block has at least 5 step nodes plus at least one GATE decision node
expectations.evidence: The diagram in §3 is an ASCII-art box-and-arrow diagram, not a Mermaid block. There is no ```mermaid fence anywhere in the file. There is also no GATE/decision-diamond node — just a linear 5-step pipeline (DETECT → CAPTURE → TRIAGE → PROMOTE → VERIFY) drawn in plain text.

expectations.text: Edge Cases include conflicting signals (two corrections disagree) or equivalent
expectations.evidence: There is no 'Edge Cases' section in the spec. The closest content is §3.5 VERIFY (overcorrection check) and §3.3 bucket C (disambiguation needed when 'two rules tied'), but neither directly addresses two corrections that disagree about the same signature. Bucket C is about runtime ambiguity, not conflicting corrections. No explicit conflict-resolution edge case.

expectations.text: Build handoff names Python-specific capture mechanisms (decorators, context managers, structured logging)
expectations.evidence: §5 describes a Python CLI with subcommands but does not name Python-specific capture mechanisms. No mention of decorators, context managers, or structured logging libraries. The spec mentions 'append-only jsonl' and 'flock' but these are generic. The session-notes mention 'stdlib + PyYAML' and a 'RulesStore class' but still no decorators/context-managers/structured-logging callouts. Note: the baseline did write a 600-line feedback_loop.py — but the assertion is about the build handoff in the spec, not the implementation.

expectations.text: Decision rules are testable on input alone
expectations.evidence: §3.3 TRIAGE heuristics are explicitly testable on the correction record alone: (1) recurrence — count of similar corrections in 90 days, (2) signal cleanliness — presence of strong low-collision signal, (3) user note — presence of 'always go here' string. All three are computable from the correction.jsonl record + history. §3.5 VERIFY's ≥90% replay assertion is also a deterministic test on inputs.

expectations.text: Session notes describe each of the 8 phases
expectations.evidence: session-notes.md describes the baseline's design approach, not 8 phases. It mentions DMAIC's 5 letters and the spec's 5-phase pipeline (DETECT/CAPTURE/TRIAGE/PROMOTE/VERIFY), but does not walk through the 8 phases of the process-design skill (which the baseline did not have). Phase coverage in session-notes is structural narrative, not phase-by-phase.

notes: With-skill mean pass rate 100% vs baseline 40% (delta +0.60) — large structural gap; baselines fail YAML/Mermaid/verify_spec.py because they don't follow the process-design template.
Time cost: with-skill 636s vs baseline 190s (delta +446.1s) — ~3.3x. Token cost: 122895 vs 50704 (delta +72191, ~2.4x).

All three with-skill runs hit 100% on assertions and 40/40 on verify_spec.py --final. Adversarial qa-agents reviews surfaced 30 findings total (severity 33-34 each), all routable as iteration-2 fixes — no blockers.

Repeated finding across all 3 with-skill runs: Phase 4 fan-out and Phase 7 qa-agents both fell back to in-context simulation because Task tool wasn't reachable in this runtime. The fallback works structurally but adversarial isolation collapses. This is a real environmental limitation, not a skill defect — but the skill should make the simulation-vs-real distinction sharper in telemetry and Verification Record so a downstream consumer can tell which mode ran.

Baseline outputs were substantively useful (38–54% pass on content assertions) but failed every structural conformance check. The skill's value is concentrated in: structural completeness, build-target-aware handoff, deterministic verification.

Confirmed bug surfaced by the eval (eval-1 with_skill session notes): verify_spec.py controllable-input regex is lazy-matched and crosses input boundaries — author had to reorder inputs to dodge it. Real defect to fix in iteration 2.
